texts.full_text: --- author: - | [^1]\ Depts. of Physics and Mathematics\ Humboldt University, Berlin\ E-mail: title: 'The corolla polynomial: a graph polynomial on half-edges' --- Introduction ============ The computation of a gauge theory suffers the enormous number of integrals one is confronted with. This results from the fact that the number of Feynman graphs contributing to a given amplitude is much bigger than in a scalar field theory, and from the fact that the spin structure of the Feynman rules gives a much more complicated tensorial structure. Hence, the reduction to master integrals is a much more cumbersome undertaking. Here we report on recent progress connecting the integrand for a connected amplitudeThe paper discusses recent advancements in computing connected amplitudes within quantum field theory, particularly focusing on the use of a graph polynomial called the corolla polynomial. This tool is significant for managing intricate Feynman diagrams encountered in gauge theories due to their high complexity from multiple propagators and spin structures. Key Points: 1. Graph Polynomials: The introduction highlights that while traditional methods like integration by parts or unitarity approaches are used, they can be cumbersome. Graph polynomials offer a structured approach for reduction techniques, potentially streamlining the computation

texts.full_text: {1}{\pi}$ by $w=\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}-\fIntroduction continues: $$ w = \sqrt{\frac{1}{4} - \left(\frac{l-2/\pi}{2}\right)^2} $$ with dimensions \( l = \frac{1}{\pi} \) by \( w=\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{{\pi^{2}-4}} \), whose area is approximately 0.12274. More recently, Füredi and Wetzel [@Furedi-Wetzel] demonstrated that one corner of this rectangle can be clipped to form a pentagonal cover with an even smaller area of about \( 0.11224 \). In their construction, they showed how the rectangular region could be modified by removing a small triangular section from its vertex while still maintaining coverage for all closed unit arcs. In our work presented here, we further refine this result and show that clipping another corner opposite to where Füredi and Wetzel clipped theirs results in an irregular hexagonal cover with area less than \( 0.11023 \). This new construction represents the smallest known covering region for all closed unit arcs as of now. Our approach involves a detailed analysis of geometric constraints imposed by the requirement that every possible orientation, size (unit length), and position of any given arc must fit within our proposed cover shape without overlap or gaps. We explore how removing specific triangular sections from corners affects coverage properties while ensuring minimal area is achieved through careful optimization techniques applied to this constraint set. The remainder of this paper will detail the construction process for achieving such a hexagonal configuration, provide rigorous proofs validating its effectiveness as an optimal covering region compared against previous results, and discuss implications

texts.full_text: seen this before? I inherited a bandsaw from my grandfather and I’m in the process of cleaning it up. I noticed the inside edge of the drive pulley would grind against the motor housing once every revolution. I took off the drive pulley to examine it…it doesn’t have any set screw to hold it in place. Only a square key keeps it positioned on the shaft. After replacing the drive pulley, I noticed that when I turn the saw on the pulley starts working its way off the end of the drive shaft (away from the motor). I’m afraid to let it continue running to see if it will work itself off completely, but the pulley certainly appears to be headed all the way off. I’d be grateful for any ideas anyone has on correcting this problem. The “drill and tap” option might work, but the drive shaft isn’t long enough for the collar option. The pulley extends about an inch past the end of the shaft even when the pulley’s snug (and rubbing) against the motor housing. I've considered welding a stop or shoulder onto the driveshaft to prevent it from coming loose, but that seems like overkill and would likely require some precision machining which is beyond my skill budget! texts.full_text: the following year succeeded to the title of Viscount Strangford in the Peerage of Ireland. He had literary tastes, and in 1803 published Poems from the Portuguese of Camoēns, with Remarks and Notes, Byron at this time describing him as "Hibernian Strangford". Diplomatic career Ambassador to Portugal In 1806, he served as chargé d'affaires under the Earl of Rosslyn and the Earl of St Vincent, the Extraordinary Envoys of the United Kingdom to Portugal. In 1807, he was appointed British Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary to Portugal under the reign of King George III. In 1807, as Britain's envoy to Portugal, Lord Strangford coordinated the Portuguese royal family's flight from Portugal to Brazil. Lord Clinton, as he was known in Brazil, he arrived with the Royal Family in Salvador in January 1808 and soon they moved to Rio de Janeiro where they arrived on 8 March 1808. Lord Clinton and the Brazilian accountant Dom Fernando José de Portugal had a hard work to do in the Brazilian Imperial Palace. They had to raise the money moved from Portugal to Brazil under the English escort. Their work was during thirty days. The tax service of 2% was according to the Prize Money (the law had been cancelled in 1803 and was re-edited in 1807). They counted one hundred million Pounds and two million pounds in taxes. (In that year, with that money would be possible to buy two hundred million bags of coffee, nowadays it is U$20 billion). After that, the payment delayed fourteen years to be paid after the English recognizance of the Brazilian Independence. That was the money Napoleon wanted to finance his war against England. Napoleon said in his memoirs that Don Jon was the only one to trick him. Ambassador to Sweden He was British Envoy Ext

texts.full_text: rld of Flying Lotus. Through a series of interviews, we hear from those closest to Ellison, shedding light on the persona of Captain Murphy and the impact he has had on the world of music. texts.full_text: tation Now About SAEN About Michael Budkie About SAEN: Leading the Fight Stop Animal Exploitation NOW! (SAEN) was founded in 1996 to force an end to the abuse of animals in laboratories. Our first major event was a news conference that revealed the suffering endured by dogs, rabbits, and primates in 9 laboratories across the United States. Documents obtained by SAEN had revealed severe abuses within the laboratories of Michigan State University, University of Southern California, University of Washington, Stolle Research and Development, University of South Florida, Yerkes Regional Primate Research Center (associated with Emory University), University of Florida, University of Pennsylvania, and the University of Toledo. The USDA fined several of these laboratories, and abusive primate experiments within the laboratories were halted as a direct result. Since its inception, SAEN has been at the forefront of advocating for ethical alternatives to animal testing and exposing cruel practices in laboratory settings. Through their relentless pursuit of justice and compassion towards animals used in scientific research, they have initiated numerous campaigns that bring attention to unethical treatment within laboratories across America. In addition to raising awareness through news conferences, investigations into questionable experimental methods continue regularly under SAEN’s scrutiny. By gathering evidence from animal caretakers who are willing or forced to report mistreatment firsthand and by reviewing publicly accessible records pertaining to laboratory protocols compliance issues can arise which often lead to legal actions taken against institutions involved in illegal practices. The organization also plays a critical role within legislative processes aimed at reforming regulations governing experimentation on animals. By lobbying governmental entities, providing expert testimonies before lawmakers during committee hearings on animal welfare bills and educating policymakers about the necessity of transitioning away from invasive techniques towards non-animal alternatives wherever possible—SAEN ensures that ethical considerations remain central in ongoing scientific progress. Moreover, SAEN collaborates with other like-minded organizations worldwide to build a global network advocating for better treatment standards within laboratory practices. This collective action contributes significantly toward advancing public sentiment concerning the importance of animal rights an

texts.full_text: Rajasthan Phephana (also Fefana) is a village in Hanumanagar District of the Indian state of Rajasthan. It lies within Sikrai Tehsil and forms part of Bhatinda Dak bungalow panchayat samiti. The region has predominantly agricultural land use, with crops like wheat being cultivated extensively due to its favorable climate conditions during specific seasons in this semi-arid zone. Fefana is located relatively close major cities such as Hanumangarh city and the state capital of Jaipur but retains a quieter rural character that contributes significantly to local traditions. The village plays an essential role within regional infrastructure, facilitating both socio-economic activities for its inhabitants while supporting surrounding areas through services related primarily with agriculture. In terms of connectivity, it benefits from nearby road networks linking larger cities which enable better access and economic opportunities beyond the traditional scope confined only by rural limitations. Culturally significant festivals like Diwali are celebrated enthusiastically in Fefana as they hold special importance to local communities here just across Rajasthan's vibrant cultural tapestry. Moreover, regional governance includes Panchayat Raj institutions that manage civic amenities such schooling and healthcare facilities. texts.full_text: plans. In 2004 a Senate committee recommended creating national drug plan as part of medicare (1). The idea was endorsed again last year when the Health Council of Canada released its final report, calling it “the most important health care reform issue facing governments in this country”(2). The Canadian Medical Association has also called for universal pharmacare. In a recent editorial published on Healthy Debate and elsewhere Drs Irfan Dhalla & Andreas Laupacis argued that national drug plan would be good public policy (3). They wrote: “Canadians pay more per capita than any other country with the exception of Americans, who have no formulary at all… A universal pharmacare program could save Canadians billions annually by reducing waste and inefficiency in our current system.” But not everyone agrees. In a recent article published on Healthy Debate Drs Michael Rachlis & Danielle Martin argued that national drug plan would be bad public policy (4). They wrote: “National Pharmacare is an idea whose time has come, but only if it’s done right… The most important thing to get straight about pharmacare in Canada today: we already have a system of universal coverage for prescription drugs. It just doesn’t work very well.” In this debate Drs Dhalla & Laupacis argue that national drug plan would be good public policy, while Rachlis and Martin counter with their own arguments. texts.full_text: s likely due to an invalid pointer being passed to `LocalFree()` or a problem with how memory was allocated for the security descriptor. Here are some common reasons why this might be happening: 1. NULL Pointer: If `GetNamedSecurityInfo` fails, it may not allocate any memory and leave `pSD` as NULL. Calling `LocalFree(NULL)` is safe but calling `LocalFree()` with an invalid pointer can cause a crash. 2. Memory Allocation Mismatch: The security descriptor returned by `GetNamedSecurityInfo` might be allocated in such a way that it should not be freed using `LocalFree()`. For example, if the memory was allocated differently (e.g., on the stack or statically), freeing it with `LocalFree()` can cause issues. 3. Double Free: If you accidentally free the same pointer more than once, this will lead to undefined behavior and likely a crash. 4. Corrupted Memory: The security descriptor might be corrupted if there is an issue elsewhere in your code that writes outside allocated memory boundaries or overwrites pointers. To debug this further: 1. Check Return Values: Ensure `GetNamedSecurityInfo` returns successfully before attempting to free the resources. 2. texts.full_text: I was barely functional. When I came out of it, I still had the knee pain and now it was worse. I originally only experienced knee pain going down the stairs. Now Iexperienced it going up or down them. It was much more preferable than the hip problems, sure, but it was still very aggravating. I developed a bizarre fear of stairs because I knew that it would hurt. It didn’t matter if it was 3 flights of stairs or just 1 lone stair, it would hurt. Eventually I just sucked it upand resigned myself to the idea that it would hurt but I still had to do it. I live around stairs. There are stairs where I live. Thereare stairs where I work. There are stairs, curbs, sidewalks, and other things to step onto or off of almosteverywhere you go. Sure, there is usually a spot for wheelchairs or shopping carts where it is a small ramp instead of stairs, but that isn’t the case everywhere. It also began to hurt when standing up or sitting down. It hurt more oftenthan not, any time I had to use my knees. Summary Quite a mess, right? I know that’s long, but I wanted to give you an idea of just how far down I am coming up from. When I read about people who have 30 extra pounds and are complaining about how they feel, I want to smack them. And then, I want to smack myself. texts.full_text: is not inert: role of metal ions released by biocorrosion in aseptic loosening--current concepts. Metal implants are essential therapeutic tools for the treatment of bone fractures and joint replacements. The metals and metal alloys used in contemporary orthopediMetal implants are essential therapeutic tools for the treatment of bone fractures and joint replacements. The metals and metal alloys used in contemporary orthopedic implants, such as stainless steel, cobalt-chromium, and titanium alloys, are chosen for their biocompatibility, mechanical strength, and corrosion resistance. However, despite their robust properties, these materials are not entirely inert within the biological environment. Biocorrosion, the degradation of metal implants due to biological and chemical processes, can lead to the release of metal ions and particles. This phenomenon is of significant concern in the context of aseptic loosening, a common complication in joint replacement surgeries. Aseptic loosening refers to the failure of the implant-bone interface without the presence of infection, often resulting in implant failure and the need for revision surgery. The role of metal ions released by biocorrosion in aseptic loosening is multifaceted. Firstly, metal ions can induce an inflammatory response in the surrounding tissue. This response is characterized by the activation of immune cells, such as macrophages, which release pro-inflammatory cytokines. These cytokines can stimulate the production of osteoclasts, cells responsible for bone resorption, leading to bone loss around the implant. Secondly, metal ions can directly affect bone cells. For example, cobalt and chromium ions have been shown to inhibit osteoblast activity, the cells responsible for bone formation, and to promote osteoclast activity. This imbalance between bone formation and resorption can further exacerbate bone loss and contribute to implant loosening. Additionally, the accumulation of metal

By MASAKO HIRSCH STAFF WRITER Loyola beamed up a number of new art pieces placed around campus to complement the city-wide international modern art show called Prospect I . The exhibit is on display until January and features works by artists from all over the world who have been commissioned to create site specific installations. “The idea was that we would be able to bring in some work, but it had to fit into our campus,” said Loyola’s Director of Art Galleries Dan Cameron. “We wanted something that could go outside.” The art pieces are located on St. Charles Avenue and the Quad. One piece is a large sculpture by artist Mark Handforth called Untitled (2008). It consists of two giant hands holding up an enormous light bulb, which sits in front of Monroe Hall. Another installation features 15 sculptures made from recycled materials that were placed around campus on Oct. 9 and will remain until the end of Prospect I . The pieces are by artist Willie Birch who is a New Orleans native. “The idea was to have them be like little aliens,” Cameron said, referring to how they look as if they came from outer space. The event is being held June 4 from 5:30-7:30 p.m. in the Red-wood Area Hospital education room. The Redwood Area Hospital invites all community cancer survivors to join in celebrating life at the 19th annual cancer survivors picnic. The event is being held June 4 from 5:30-7:30 p.m. in the Red-wood Area Hospital education room. The picnic is a chance for cancer survivors, caregivers, family members, friends and healthcare professionals to join together to show life after a cancer diagnosis can be meaningful and productive. “Let’s join together and show the community that overcoming challenges like surviving cancer isn’t just possible; it's empowering,” said Dr. Laura Martinez of Redwood Area Hospital. The picnic will feature light refreshments, guest speakers sharing their stories of resilience, informative sessions about ongoing support resources for survivors, as well opportunities to connect with local healthcare providers and advocacy groups dedicated to improving survivor care experiences in the region. Attendees are encouraged not only from those who have personally faced cancer but also friends or family members looking to show solidarity. This event is a chance for all attendees—survivors included—to reflect on their journeys, share encouragement, celebrate milestones achieved since diagnosis as well find inspiration and new resources that support living life fully post-treatment. texts.full_text: (AUC=0.89) and MSE (MSE is not typically used as a primary metric for evaluating classification models, especially when dealing with imbalanced datasets like yours where one class significantly outnumbers the other. Instead, metrics such as precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC are more commonly used because they provide better insights into how well your model performs on both classes. Given that you have very good results in terms of AUC (0.89), this suggests that your logistic regression model is reasonably effective at distinguishing between the two classes, particularly when it comes to ranking positive instances higher than negative ones. However, there are a few points to consider: 1. Class Imbalance and Metrics: - Precision: Measures how many of the predicted positives (Y=1) were actually correct. - Recall: Measures how many of the actual positives (Y=1) were correctly identified by your model. - F1-Score: The harmonic mean of precision and recall, which provides a balance between them. 2. Overfitting: - Since you are using SMOTE to oversample the minority class in your training set, there is a risk that your model might overfit to these synthetic samples. This can lead to overly optimistic performance metrics on the training data but poor generalization to unseen test data. - To mitigate this, consider cross-validation and ensuring that the same preprocessing steps (including SMOTE) are applied consistently in each fold. 3. Threshold Adjustment: - The default threshold for classification is typically 0.5, meaning any predicted probability above 0.5 will be classified as Y=1. However, with imbalanced datasets, this might not always yield the best results. - You can experiment with different thresholds to optimize precision and recall based on your specific needs. 4.
